---
title: "Git & GitHub at emLab"
subtitle: "A hands-on team demo: branches · pull requests · code review · merge conflicts"
author: "Environmental Markets Lab (emLab)"
date: today
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/emlab_logo_horizontal.svg
  data-background-size: 22%
  data-background-position: 50% 88%
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emlab-demo.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    history: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    width: 1280
    height: 800
    footer: "<img src='images/emlab_logo_horizontal.svg' class='footer-logo'> Git & GitHub demo"
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

## How this demo works

We will move back and forth between **these slides** and **live coding**.

::: {.panel}
- The slides contain **instructions** for live-coding.
- We will jump between **github.com**, **Positron**, **RStudio**, and the **terminal**.
- Everyone is working in the **same shared practice repo** today.
:::

By the end you will have practiced emLab Git workflow actions:
- branch
- commit
- open a PR
- review a PR
- merge
- work with issues
- resolve a merge conflict

::: {.notes}
Set expectations: this is hands-on. Tell people to keep the slides open in one
window and their IDE in another. Confirm everyone has been added to the shared
repo and can see it on github.com before starting.
:::

## Reading the slides {.smaller}

Every interactive slide tells you **who acts** and **where**.

**Who:**

- [WATCH]{.badge-watch} — the presenters do this live; everyone follows on screen. No interactive component.
- [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} — do this now, on your own machine.

**Where:**

- [github.com]{.tool-gh}  [Positron]{.tool-pos}  [RStudio]{.tool-rs}  [VS Code]{.tool-vs}  [Terminal]{.tool-term}

::: {.panel}
**Several tools, one workflow.** Most people use **Positron** or **RStudio**;
every git step can also be done from the **terminal**. We show the options where
they differ.
:::

## Today's roadmap {.smaller .tight}

1. Protect `main` with a ruleset  [WATCH]{.badge-watch}
2. Clone the repo  [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
3. Set up the **Air** formatter  [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
4. Create an **issue**, then **branch from it**  [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
5. Make a change → **commit** → **push**  [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
6. Open a **pull request** & assign a reviewer  [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
7. **Code review — Example 1:** request changes → revise → approve → merge (closes issue) → delete [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
8. **Code review — Example 2:** new branch → reviewer edits, approves & merges → delete [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}
9. **Merge conflict** — create one on purpose, then resolve it [EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone}

# 1 · Protect `main` {.divider}

Watch setup. You'll feel it later when a direct push to `main` is refused.

## Create a branch ruleset {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[WATCH]{.badge-watch} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh} &nbsp; Settings → Rules → Rulesets
:::

Repo: **Settings → Rules → Rulesets → New ruleset → New branch ruleset**.

- **Name ruleset:** `protect-main`
- **Add to Bypass list (optional):** `protect-main`
- **Enable status:** `Active`
- **Target branches:** add target → `Default` (or pattern `~DEFAULT_BRANCH`)
- **Rules — turn on:**
  - **Restrict deletions**
  - **Require a pull request before merging** → require **1** approval
  - **Block force pushes**

::: {.panel}
emLab **recommends** protecting `main` —
it's not strictly required, but it's best practice and we'll use it today.
:::

::: {.notes}
Optionally show the Copilot code review toggle under Settings → Copilot, and
mention it's a useful first pass but does not replace human review. Keep the
bypass list empty for the demo so everyone hits the protection.
:::

## Prove it works

::: {.where}
[WATCH]{.badge-watch} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} [Terminal]{.tool-term}
:::

Attempt to push directly to `main`.

Terminal:
```bash
git switch main
# make a trivial edit, commit it, then:
git push
```

With the ruleset on, the push is **rejected**.

That error is the ruleset doing its job. From here on, **everything goes
through a branch and a PR.**

# 2 · Clone the repo {.divider}

Everyone gets a local copy of the shared repo and links it to GitHub.

## Copy the repo URL

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

On the shared repo, click the green **Code** button:

- **HTTPS** tab if you authenticate with a Personal Access Token (PAT)
- **SSH** tab if you set up SSH keys

Copy the URL with the copy icon.

```
# HTTPS
https://github.com/emlab-ucsb/sop-demo.git
# SSH
git@github.com:emlab-ucsb/sop-demo.git
```

::: {.notes}
If anyone isn't authenticated yet, point them to the SOP "Getting set up" page
(PAT via usethis::create_github_token(), then gitcreds::gitcreds_set()). Have a
backup plan for people who get stuck so the group isn't blocked.
:::

## Clone it locally {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs} / [Terminal]{.tool-term}
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Positron**

`File → New Folder from Git…`

Paste the URL, pick a destination (e.g. `~/github/`), click **OK**.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**RStudio**

`File → New Project… → Version Control → Git`

Paste the URL, choose where to store it, **Create Project**.
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
**Terminal**

```bash
cd ~/github
git clone <paste-url>
cd REPO-NAME
```
:::
:::

::: {.panel}
Store repos in a dedicated folder like `~/github/`. **Avoid** cloud-synced
folders (Google Drive, iCloud, Dropbox) — they cause Git sync conflicts.
:::

# 3 · Set up the Air formatter {.divider}

Formatting with **Air** (R) is an emLab standard. Set it up once. We'll see it
work in Section 5.

## Install Air & format on save {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [VS Code]{.tool-vs} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs}
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Positron / VS Code**

1. Extensions (`Cmd+Shift+X`) → search **"Air"** (by Posit) → Install.
2. Open user settings JSON (`Cmd+Shift+P` → *Open User Settings (JSON)*) and add:

```json
{
  "[r]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "posit.air",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  }
}
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**RStudio**

Air isn't tightly integrated. Use the `styler` package:

```r
install.packages("styler")
```

Then **Addins → Style active file** before each commit, and enable
*Tools → Global Options → Code → Saving → Ensure source files end with a
newline*.
:::
:::

::: {.panel}
We'll watch Air reformat code on save when we make our first change in
Section 5 — no need to test it now.
:::

# 4 · Issue → branch {.divider}

Issues track work alongside the code. We'll create one, then branch directly
from it so the branch and PR are linked to the issue.

## Create & triage an issue {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh} &nbsp; Issues → New issue
:::

Create an issue for the small task you'll do today:

- **Title:** *"Add [name]'s greeting function"*
- **Body:** what & why, plus a checklist:

```markdown
- [ ] Add greet() to team/yourname.R
- [ ] Have it return a greeting for a given name
```

In the sidebar set: **Assignee** (yourself), **Label** (`enhancement`),
optionally a **Milestone**.

::: {.notes}
Show how the checklist renders as interactive checkboxes and shows "0 of 2" in
the issue list, and that labels make the list filterable.
:::

## Create a branch from the issue {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh} → [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs} / [Terminal]{.tool-term}
:::

On your issue, in the **Development** section (right sidebar) → **Create a
branch**. Accept the suggested name — it's auto-linked to the issue.

Now get that branch onto your machine:

- **Positron / VS Code / RStudio:** click the branch picker → **fetch/refresh**,
  then **switch** to the new branch.
- **Terminal:**

```bash
git fetch
git switch <issue-branch-name>
```

::: {.panel}
Creating the branch from the issue links them: branch activity shows in the
issue timeline, and the issue will close automatically when the PR merges.
:::

# 5 · Change → commit → push {.divider}

Make a small change on your branch, commit it, and push it to GitHub.

## Make a change {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs}
:::

Create a new file **named after you** so we don't overlap — e.g.
`team/yourname.R`:

```r
# team/tracey.R
greet <- function(name) {
  paste0("The best state in the nation, New Jersey, welcomes you, ", name)
}

greet("Danielle")
```

Type it messily first (no spaces, odd indents), then **save** and watch **Air**
reformat it to Tidyverse style.

::: {.panel}
Working in your own file avoids accidental conflicts. We'll create a conflict
*on purpose* later, in a shared file.
:::

## Commit your work {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs} (or [Terminal]{.tool-term})
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="56%"}
In the **Source Control** panel (Positron/VS Code) or **Git** pane (RStudio):

1. **Stage** `team/yourname.R` (click the **+** next to the file).
2. Type a clear commit **message**.
3. Click **Commit**.

Prefer the terminal?

```bash
git add team/yourname.R
git commit -m "Add Tracey's greeting function"
```
:::
::: {.column width="44%"}
![](images/git_commit_2x.png){fig-align="center"}

::: {.caption}
Write messages your future self can read. Source: xkcd.com/1296
:::
:::
:::

## Push your branch to GitHub {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs} (or [Terminal]{.tool-term})
:::

- **Positron / VS Code:** click **Publish Branch** (first push) or the **sync**
  arrows in the Source Control panel.
- **RStudio:** click the **Push** (green up-arrow) in the Git pane.

Prefer the terminal?

```bash
git push -u origin <your-branch>
```

::: {.panel}
The `-u` (terminal only) sets upstream tracking. The IDE buttons handle this for
you the first time you publish a branch.
:::

# 6 · Open a pull request {.divider}

Propose your branch for merging into `main`, link it to your issue, and ask your
partner to review it.

## Open the PR {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

After pushing, GitHub shows a **"Compare & pull request"** banner — click it
(or **Pull requests → New pull request**).

- **Base:** `main`  ·  **Compare:** your branch
- **Title:** summarize the change: *"Add greeting function"*
- **Description:** what it does and why, plus **`Closes #<issue-number>`**.

::: {.panel}
Branching from the issue *links* the two, but that link alone won't close it.
The **`Closes #N`** keyword is what actually **closes the issue when the PR
merges** — so add it here. (Keep PRs small and focused; not ready yet? Open a
**Draft PR**.)
:::

## Assign your reviewer {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

**Pair up: A reviews B, and B reviews A.**

In your PR's right sidebar → **Reviewers** → choose your partner. Our ruleset
requires **1 approval** before the PR can merge, so your partner's review is
what unblocks the merge.

::: {.panel}
*(Optional: **Assignees** marks who owns/will merge the PR — you can assign
yourself. The important one today is **Reviewer** = your partner.)*
:::

::: {.notes}
Make sure pairs are set before moving on — the review sections all depend on each
person having their partner assigned as reviewer on an open PR.
:::

## What a reviewer is actually doing {.smaller}

::: {.where}
Concept — applies to every review below
:::

GitHub gives three review outcomes:

- **Comment** — feedback, no decision.
- **Approve** — ready to merge.
- **Request changes** — specific things must be fixed first.

::: {.panel .tight}
Good review habits (from the emLab Code Review SOP):

- Be **specific and constructive** — suggest a fix, don't just flag.
- Mark **"Required:"** vs **"Suggestion:"/"Nit:"** so priorities are clear.
- Prioritize **correctness** over style.
- For accuracy/reproducibility, **actually run the code**.
- Be **timely** — a stale PR blocks your partner.
:::

# Example 1 {.divider}

Reviewer **requests changes** → author **revises** → reviewer **approves** →
**merge** (which **closes the issue**) → **delete branch**.

## 1a · Reviewer requests changes {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} (as reviewer) &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

Open your **partner's** PR → **Files changed** tab.

- Hover a line → click the blue **+** → leave a **line comment**, for example:
  - *"Required: make the greeting friendlier — e.g., add an exclamation point."*
- Click **Review changes** (top right) → choose **Request changes** → **Submit review**.

![](images/exclamation-point.jpeg){width="280" fig-align="center"}

::: {.panel}
"Request changes" blocks the merge until you re-review — use it when you want
another look. We'll use **Approve** in Example 2 to contrast.
:::

## 1b · Author makes the change {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} (as author) &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} → [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

Back on **your own branch**, make the simple change your reviewer asked for:

```r
greet <- function(name) {
  paste0("The best state in the nation, New Jersey, welcomes you, ", name, "! We can't wait for you to taste a bagel.")
}
```

Save (Air formats it), then **commit and push** from the Source Control / Git
panel — the PR updates automatically.

On GitHub, **Resolve conversation** on the thread, then **re-request review**.

## 1c · Reviewer approves, author merges {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

Reviewer: re-open the PR → **Review changes → Approve → Submit**.

Author: the green **Merge pull request** is now enabled → **Confirm merge**.

::: {.panel}
Because your PR said **`Closes #N`**, merging it **auto-closes your issue** —
check the Issues tab to confirm. Merge strategies: **Create a merge commit**
(default today), **Squash and merge**, or **Rebase and merge**.
:::

## 1d · Delete the merged branch {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh} (+ IDE)
:::

Right after merging, GitHub shows **Delete branch** — click it. This deletes the
branch **on GitHub** (the remote copy).

Now clean up on your machine:

1. **Switch to `main` and pull** — Positron/RStudio: pick `main` in the branch
   picker, click **pull/sync**. This brings the merged work down.
2. **Delete your local branch** — it still exists locally until you remove it:
   - **Positron / VS Code:** `Cmd+Shift+P` → **Git: Delete Branch…** → pick it.
   - **RStudio / Terminal:** `git branch -d <your-branch>`

::: {.panel}
There are always **two copies** of a branch — remote (GitHub) and local (your
machine). Deleting one doesn't delete the other.
:::

# Example 2 {.divider}

A lighter-touch review: **create a new branch (not from an issue)**, then the reviewer
**makes a small edit themselves** and **approves and merges**.

## 2a · Create a new branch {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; any tool — start from an up-to-date `main`
:::

::: {.panel}
However you create it, a branch always starts from your current `main` — so make
sure `main` is up to date first.
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Positron / VS Code**

Click the branch name (bottom-left) → **Create new branch…** → name it
`yourname-add-farewell`.

**RStudio**

Git pane → branch icon (**New Branch**) → name it → Create.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**github.com**

Branch dropdown → type the new name → **Create branch**, then fetch/switch to it
in your IDE.

**Terminal**

```bash
git switch main && git pull
git switch -c yourname-add-farewell
```
:::
:::

## 2b · Change, then reviewer edits & merges {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [Positron]{.tool-pos} → [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

Author: add a second function, commit, push, and open a PR (assign your partner):

```r
farewell <- function(name) {
  paste0("Goodbye, ", name, "!")
}
```

Reviewer: a tiny fix you'd rather just make yourself?

1. **Files changed** → click the **pencil ✏️** to edit the file in the browser.
2. Tweak it → **Commit changes** to the PR's branch.
3. **Review changes → Approve → Submit**, then **Merge pull request**.

::: {.panel}
This branch wasn't created from an issue, so there's nothing to close. If you
*did* want a PR to close an issue, add **`Closes #<issue-number>`** to its
description — that keyword is the reliable way to auto-close on merge, no matter
how the branch was created. (A reviewer committing a *small* fix is fine; for
anything substantial, comment so the author keeps ownership.)
:::

## 2c · Delete the branch — local vs. remote {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh} + [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [RStudio]{.tool-rs}
:::

Same as Example 1 — there are **two copies**, and you delete each separately:

- **Remote (GitHub):** click **Delete branch** right after the merge.
- **Local (your machine):** switch to `main`, **pull/sync**, then delete the
  local branch:
  - **Positron / VS Code:** `Cmd+Shift+P` → **Git: Delete Branch…** → pick the
    merged branch.
  - **RStudio / Terminal:** `git branch -d <your-branch>` *(RStudio has no
    delete-branch button — use the Terminal tab)*.

::: {.panel}
`git branch -d` is the safe form — it refuses to delete a branch that hasn't been
merged yet, so you can't lose unmerged work.
:::

# 7 · Merge conflicts {.divider}

Two changes to the **same line** can't auto-merge. Let's create one on purpose,
then resolve it using the IDE and github.com.

## Set up the conflict — with your partner {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} (in your pairs) &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

A conflict needs **two people editing the same line**, so do this **with your
partner** in a **shared file** — e.g. `sop-demo/partner-files/tracey-danielle.R`.

1. Decide who is **Partner A** and **Partner B**. 
2. **Partner A**: Create a new branch to make file that that you will both work on.
```r
## tracey and danielle file

activity_message <- function(activity) {
  paste0("I went on one ", activity, ", it's summer!")
}

```
3. **Partner A**: Commit, push, open PR.
4. **Partner B**: Review, approve, submit.
5. **Partner A**: Merge, delete branch.
6. **Partners A and B**: Update `main` and create separate branches from `main`
7. **Partner A**: Make a change. Commit, push, open PR. 
8. **Partner B**: Review, approve, submit, merge. Delete branch.
9. **Partner B**: *On your branch*, modify the code. Commit, push, open PR.

Now `main` (A's code) and Partner B's branch changed the **same
line** differently → GitHub can't auto-merge B's PR. **Partner B resolves it.**

::: {.notes}
Each pair gets its own pre-seeded file (R/pair-1.R, R/pair-2.R, …) so conflicts
stay contained to two people instead of the whole room fighting over one file.
Partner A must merge before Partner B opens their PR. Then partners swap roles so
everyone resolves one.
:::

## Resolve it on github.com {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} &nbsp; [github.com]{.tool-gh}
:::

Open **Partner B's** PR. It shows **"This branch has conflicts that must be
resolved."** Click **Resolve conflicts** to open the web editor:

```r
<<<<<<< your-branch-name
  paste0("I went on a ", activity, " — best summer ever!")
=======
  paste0("I went on one ", activity, ", it's summer!")
>>>>>>> main
```

Delete the `<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>` markers, keep the **one** correct
line, then **Mark as resolved → Commit merge**. The PR is now mergeable.

::: {.panel}
GitHub labels the sides with the **branch names** (top = your branch, bottom =
`main`). It does *not* say "HEAD" — those raw markers only show in a local file.
:::

## Resolve it in Positron / VS Code {.smaller}

::: {.where}
Alternative — [Positron]{.tool-pos} / [VS Code]{.tool-vs}
:::

If you merged `main` locally (`Git: Merge Branch…`), the conflicted file opens in
the **merge editor** with buttons:

- **Accept Current Change** — keep *your branch's* version.
- **Accept Incoming Change** — keep the version coming from `main`.
- **Accept Both** — keep both, in order.

Pick the right option (or hand-edit), **save**, then **stage** the file and
**commit** the merge in the Source Control panel, and **push/sync**.

::: {.panel}
"Current" = your branch · "Incoming" = `main`. That's the same idea as the
`<<<<<<<` / `>>>>>>>` markers, just with buttons.
:::

## Keep your branch up to date with `main` {.smaller}

::: {.where}
[EVERYONE]{.badge-everyone} — do this regularly, *not* just when there's a conflict
:::

The best way to avoid a painful conflict is to pull `main`'s changes into your
branch **often** while you work. The longer a branch drifts from `main`, the
harder the eventual merge.

::: {.panel}
**Watch out:** clicking **Pull / Sync** only updates your *current* branch from
its own remote — it does **not** bring `main`'s new commits into your branch. For
that you must **merge `main` into your branch**.
:::

How to merge `main` in (after switching to `main` and pulling so it's current):

- **Positron / VS Code:** `Cmd+Shift+P` → **Git: Merge Branch…** → pick `main`.
- **Terminal:** `git switch your-branch` then `git merge main`.

Also: keep branches **short-lived and focused**, keep PRs **small**, and
**coordinate** when two people touch the same file.

# Recap {.divider}

## What you did today {.smaller .tight}

- ✅ Protected `main` with a **ruleset** (PR + 1 review)
- ✅ **Cloned** the shared repo
- ✅ Set up the **Air** formatter (format on save)
- ✅ Created and triaged an **issue**, then **branched from it**
- ✅ Made a change, **committed**, and **pushed** (from the IDE)
- ✅ Opened a **PR** (`Closes #N`) and assigned a **reviewer**
- ✅ **Example 1:** requested changes → revised → approved → merged (**closed the issue**) → deleted
- ✅ **Example 2:** new branch → reviewer edited → approved → merged → deleted
- ✅ Created and **resolved a merge conflict**

::: {.panel}
This is the everyday emLab workflow: code reaches `main` through branch-based
tasks, pull requests, and review.
:::

## Where to go next

::: {.panel}
The full **emLab Standard Operating Procedures** cover all of this in depth:
:::

- **Getting set up** — git install, PAT/SSH, joining the org
- **Working with branches** · **Pull Requests** · **Using Issues** · **Rulesets**
- **Code Review** — planning, standards, and best practices
- **Code Styling** — Air & Ruff setup

📖 [emlab-ucsb.github.io/SOP](https://emlab-ucsb.github.io/SOP/)

### Questions?

::: {.notes}
Leave time for Q&A. Point people back to the SOP for anything they want to
revisit, and remind them the workflow is the same on every emLab project repo.
:::
